Chapter 1 — Your First Terminal Session: — Claude Code runs in your terminal, reads your files, runs commands, and works autonomously across multiple steps. That a
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of Your First Terminal Session: Claude Code Is Not ChatGPT, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, What the agentic loop is, Installation, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. Claude Code runs in your terminal, reads your files, runs commands, and works autonomously across multiple steps.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

The teacher opened their terminal for the first time with Claude Code running. The cursor blinked. They typed claude and

Opening — Claude opened the project folder. Read the existing files. Identified the framework (a basic HTML/CSS site, no build too
NARRATION

The teacher opened their terminal for the first time with Claude Code running. The cursor blinked. They typed claude and pressed enter. Claude opened the project folder. Read the existing files. Identified the framework (a basic HTML/CSS site, no build tool, three pages in pages/, a styles.css at the root). Proposed a structure that would let them add new pages without changing the existing ones. Asked which page to start with.

// SLIDE 03 — WHAT THE AGENTIC LOOP IS

WHAT THE AGENTIC LOOP IS.

Esc→Plan mode→Handoff conditions
NARRATION

Claude.ai is a turn-based conversation. You type. The model produces text. You read. You decide what to do. Claude Code is an agentic loop. The model gathers context , reads files, runs read-only commands, examines the project state. It takes action , edits files, runs commands, calls APIs. It verifies results , checks whether the action did what was intended, often by running another read-only command. Then it loops: gather, act, verify, gather, act, verify. The whole loop can run for many iterations before reporting back to you.

// SLIDE 04 — INSTALLATION

INSTALLATION

On macOS / Linux:→On Windows:→To run:→If you hit a school IT wall:
NARRATION

The exact commands. Test them on your machine before reading further.

// SLIDE 05 — QUESTIONS BEFORE CODE: FIVE QU

QUESTIONS BEFORE CODE: FIVE QUESTIONS, NO CHANGES

·"What is the file structure of this project? List the directories and files at the top two levels, and tell me what you
·"What styling approach does this project use? What CSS conventions do you see, and what would you assume if I asked you
·"If I asked you to add a 'Contact' page to this site, what files would you create or modify? Do not make any changes yet
"Are there any patterns in this project that look inconsistentplaces where similar things are done differently in different files?"
·"What is the one thing about this project that, if I do not tell you about it explicitly, you would probably get wrong o
NARRATION

Before you ask Claude to change anything, ask five questions. Each question is a probe , Claude answers, you read, you calibrate your sense of what Claude can and cannot see, what it would do without further specification, where you would have to correct it. Five questions for the class-website project from this chapter's opening:

// SLIDE 06 — PLAN MODE: THE DEMONSTRATION

PLAN MODE: THE DEMONSTRATION

Enter plan mode. On the keyboard: Shift+Tab twice. The CLI shows you are in plan mode (the prompt indicator changes).

Plan mode: the demonstration — Now ask Claude to propose a change without making it:
NARRATION

Enter plan mode. On the keyboard: Shift+Tab twice. The CLI shows you are in plan mode (the prompt indicator changes). Now ask Claude to propose a change without making it:

// SLIDE 07 — THE CONTEXT WINDOW: YOUR PRIMA

THE CONTEXT WINDOW: YOUR PRIMARY RESOURCE

/context→/clear→/compact focus on X
NARRATION

The single most important constraint in Claude Code is the context window. Everything Claude knows in a session lives in the context window. As the session continues, the context fills with file reads, command outputs, the conversation history, and Claude's intermediate reasoning. When the window fills, performance degrades , Claude forgets earlier parts of the session, repeats work, makes errors it would not have made earlier.

// SLIDE 08 — TEACHER BUILD: THE FIRST SESSI

TEACHER BUILD: THE FIRST SESSION

·Install Claude Code. Run the npm install. Authenticate. Verify by running claude --version.
·Create the class-website folder. mkdir ~/teaching/class-website && cd ~/teaching/class-website. If you already have a cl
·Run claude to start the session. Then /init. Read the generated CLAUDE.md. Note three things Claude noticed that you had
·Ask the five questions above (adapted to your project's specifics). Do not make any changes. Read the answers. Identify
Enter plan mode. Propose one small change to the projectadding a single page, or changing the styling approach for one section. Read the plan. Edit it with Ctrl+G. Approve.
NARRATION

Apply this chapter to your class-website project. Install Claude Code. Run the npm install. Authenticate. Verify by running claude --version.

// SLIDE 09 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS R

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: STUDENTS RUN THEIR FIRST SESSION

·Run /init. Read the generated CLAUDE.md. Note one thing Claude saw that they would not have written down themselves.
·Ask three questions about the codebase. Document Claude's answers.
Make no changes in this first session. Submit a one-paragraph reflectionwhat surprised them about Claude's reading of the project?
NARRATION

Provide a starter project , a small repo with three or four files in a structure that matches a real project shape. The repo's purpose can be anything (a small website, a CSV processing script, a simple data visualization); the point is that students run Claude Code on something with real structure, not on a one-file demo. Run /init. Read the generated CLAUDE.md. Note one thing Claude saw that they would not have written down themselves. Ask three questions about the codebase. Document Claude's answers.

// SLIDE 10 — COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

"Claude Code is just a better Claude.ai."→"I'll skip questions-before-code; I know what I want."→"Installation problems mean this book isn't for me."→"Plan mode is overhead."→"The context window is a developer concern; I'll just keep typing."
NARRATION

"Claude Code is just a better Claude.ai." No. Agentic system, file access, command execution, autonomous multi-step loop. The autonomy is the feature; the discipline is what makes the feature safe. "I'll skip questions-before-code; I know what I want." Sometimes true. Often not. The five-question session takes 10 minutes; the build it informs takes the rest of the afternoon. The economics favor the questions.

// SLIDE 11 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Alan Kay (born 1940) — American computer scientist whose 1972 Xerox PARC paper "A Personal Computer for Children of All

AI Wayback Machine — The questions-before-code rule in this chapter is Kay applied to terminal AI. The first session is not the most efficien
NARRATION

Alan Kay (born 1940) , American computer scientist whose 1972 Xerox PARC paper "A Personal Computer for Children of All Ages" introduced the concept of the Dynabook , a personal computer designed not as a faster typewriter but as a medium for exploration, a tool through which the user could think about thinking.
